coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanofiber-poly(ethylene terephthalate) nanocomposite membrane from waste materials for treatment of petroleum industry wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Bhuyan, Chinmoy
Konwar, Achyut
Bora, Prarthana
Rajguru, Parashmoni
Hazarika, Swapnali
coredata.dc:description: Petroleum industry wastewater contains high level of crude oil and other types of organic substances that can cause immense harm to the agriculture, aquatic as well as terrestrial organisms. Organic solvent resistance of membranes is very important to treat such wastewater that contains high level of organic pollutants. This work reports the designing of a superhydrophilic and organic solvent resistant nanocomposite membrane using waste bottles made of poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) and cellulosic papers. Using in-situ synthesized cellulose nanofibers we could successfully fabricate porous membranes which is not possible for bare PET matrix using water as nonsolvent. Thus, we could successfully replace methanol which was used as a suitable non-solvent in earlier reports by distilled water. We successfully used the membrane for separation of synthetic crude oil-water emulsion. The membrane showed permeability up to 98 Lm−2h−1 applying pressure of 1.5 bar. The membrane also achieved removal of more than 97 % of organic substances from a crude oil-water emulsion system. The optimum membrane also showed good thermal stability with initial degradation temperature ∼350 °C and tensile strength of 0.86 MPa. The antimicrobial property of the nanocomposite membranes could be achieved by coating its surface with carbon dots rooted graphene oxide.

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanofiber-poly(ethylene terephthalate) nanocomposite membrane from waste materials for treatment of petroleum industry wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Petroleum industry wastewater contains high level of crude oil and other types of organic substances that can cause immense harm to the agriculture, aquatic as well as terrestrial organisms. Organic solvent resistance of membranes is very important to treat such wastewater that contains high level of organic pollutants. This work reports the designing of a superhydrophilic and organic solvent resistant nanocomposite membrane using waste bottles made of poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) and cellulosic papers. Using in-situ synthesized cellulose nanofibers we could successfully fabricate porous membranes which is not possible for bare PET matrix using water as nonsolvent. Thus, we could successfully replace methanol which was used as a suitable non-solvent in earlier reports by distilled water. We successfully used the membrane for separation of synthetic crude oil-water emulsion. The membrane showed permeability up to 98 Lm−2h−1 applying pressure of 1.5 bar. The membrane also achieved removal of more than 97 % of organic substances from a crude oil-water emulsion system. The optimum membrane also showed good thermal stability with initial degradation temperature ∼350 °C and tensile strength of 0.86 MPa. The antimicrobial property of the nanocomposite membranes could be achieved by coating its surface with carbon dots rooted graphene oxide.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The growth of the petroleum industry is now one of the major sources of water pollution. Crude oil is mixed with water from the petroleum industry during its production and refining. The presence of crude oil in water bodies or in soil severely affects the nearby ecosystem (Simonovic et al., 2009). Crude oil contains various hydrocarbons like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons that have carcinogenic and genotoxic effects on living beings (Amat-Bronnert et al., 2007). Oil-based organic contaminants are consumed by the aquatic organisms present in the water body and finally reach human beings through the food chain (Aguilera et al., 2010). Various studies claim that children affected by oil exposure have a high risk of developing leukemia and acute nonlymphocytic leukemia. Also, the skin resistance of that type of child is very low. As hydrocarbon oil has carcinogenic properties, upon long-term contact with organic contaminants, one may have a high risk of developing skin tumors (Baars, 2002). Oil-based organic materials persist in the water body and are consumed by various microorganisms present in the water body, which results in a significant decrease in oxygen level in the aquatic system. Moreover, the deposition of oily substances on the soil surface reduces the water-absorbing capacity of soil, which results in a decrease in its fertility (Reddy et al., 2011). Therefore, considering the harmful effects of petroleum-based oily organic substances, proper treatment of wastewater produced by petroleum industries is of utmost importance. It will also mitigate the water scarcity problem faced by the petroleum industry because, by proper treatment, oil-based wastewater may be converted to reusable water.

In the literature, various sorbents or filtration materials in different forms, such as membranes (Duman et al., 2022a), sponges (Duman et al., 2021), and aerogel (Duman et al., 2022b) forms, have been successfully used for the separation of oil-water emulsions. Traditional methods used for the treatment of oily water, such as gravitational settling, centrifugation, floatation, coagulation and flocculation, absorption, adsorption, biological treatment, etc., fail to remove oil droplets having a size less than 20 µm. In this case, membrane-based separation plays a very significant role as it can separate the oil droplets even in the nanometer range (Zhu et al., 2014). Removal of other pollutants present in wastewater can also be achieved in a single operation by membrane technology. Moreover, easy scaling up, tunable pore size, surface property, and hence selectivity are other advantages of the membrane-based separation process (Bera et al., 2022). However, to treat such wastewater with high level of organic contaminants, the application of a superior organic solvent resistant membrane is very important for its durability. The development of chemically resistant (Xu et al., 2018; Park et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2017) and superhydrophillic membranes has still been a challenge (Ma et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2017a). Polymeric materials are the primary choice for membrane fabrication because of their high flexibility, low cost and easy fabrication process. As these synthetic polymeric materials are organic in nature, they are generally hydrophobic or oleophilic in nature. Another problem with these polymeric materials is that most of them are soluble in various organic solvents. Thus, during operation, if these membranes come in contact with organic materials, the membrane surface undergoes solubilization enhanced deformation. Hence, the pore size of the membrane gets broaden up leading to a decrease in selectivity (Marchetti et al., 2014). Therefore, the researchers are now engaged in finding a solution for making hydrophilic as well as organic solvent-resistant membranes. Most of the available reports demonstrate the use of nanomaterials with polymeric matrix forming nanocomposites (Nawaz et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2017b) or by blending with hydrophilic polymeric materials (Zhao et al., 2007) for the improvement of hydrophilicity. However, the chemical resistance property of such membranes is still a challenge. There are very few polymers that show good chemical resistance properties, whereas solubilization of such polymers becomes another challenge for synthesizing membranes from such polymers. Because the membranes are mostly synthesized by the phase inversion technique, which requires a homogenous solution of the polymer in an organic solvent (Guillen et al., 2011). In this work, we endeavored to fabricate membranes from poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET). We used waste plastic bottles as a source of PET. PET used for manufacturing of bottles is thermoplastic polyester based polymeric material. It is a copolymer of ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid (Han, 2019). Waste plastic bottles are now a major threat to the environment. Many thousand tons of plastic bottles are accumulated on the earth causing immense harm to aquatic as well as terrestrial life (Iroegbu et al., 2021). Thus, the conversion of such waste materials to a value-added product can be a big boost to the circular economy. PET has excellent chemical resistance against almost all organic solvents (Mahalingam et al., 2015). There are very few organic solvents, which can partially solubilize PET, and trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) is the only organic solvent, which acts as a good solvent for PET. Fabrication of membranes from PET bottles has already been reported by other researchers. An et al. used PET bottles for the fabrication of hydrophobic membranes by the electrospinning technique (An et al., 2019). They used such electrospun hydrophobic membranes for membrane distillation process. Pulido et al. used phase inversion technique for the fabrication of hydrophobic membranes from waste bottles for organic solvent purification (Pulido et al., 2019). A bioresource based PET nanofiltration membrane has recently been developed by Park et al., where green solvents like p-cymene and water were used for membrane fabrication (Park et al., 2021). Similar types of nanofiltration membrane based on recyclable polyester materials were developed by Hardian et al (Hardian et al., 2022). Recently Kiani et al. have reported fabrication of PET membranes from waste bottle along with Xanthan (Kiani et al., 2021). In this work, they tried to make a water purification membrane for the removal of Diltiazem from an aqueous medium. However, the hydrophilicity of the membranes could not be improved significantly even after the use of xanthan. Another disadvantage of these reported methods is the use of methanol as a suitable non-solvent in the phase inversion process. Because a huge amount of liquid media is required in the non-solvent bath in the membrane fabrication process by the phase inversion technique. In our work, we could successfully replace the methanol with water as non-solvent. To achieve this, we used cellulose nanofiber as a filler inside the PET matrix. The cellulose used here was obtained from waste paper. Cellulose is another biopolymer having very high chemical resistance. It cannot be dissolved in the common solvents (Ghasemi et al., 2017; Lindman et al., 2010; Medronho et al., 2012) Finally, we obtained a stable super hydrophilic porous membrane. Such membrane also demonstrated very good oil repelling nature proving them a very suitable candidate for oil-water separation. We could successfully demonstrate the separation of oil-water mixtures using our hydrophilic membrane which is completely fabricated from waste materials. The organic solvent resistance property is an added advantage for such membranes because apart from oil, a high amount of organic substances are also present in the oil industry wastewater (Saien and Shahrezaei, 2012). Biofouling is one of the major issues for membrane-based treatment of wastewater which reduces membrane efficiency (Zodrow et al., 2014). To address this issue, we attempted to make our fabricated membranes antimicrobial. We could successfully achieve the antimicrobial activity for the cellulose nanofiber incorporated PET membrane by coating its surface by carbon dots rooted graphene oxide. Thus, novelty of our work lies on: development of a nanocomposite membrane using PET as polymeric matrix and CNF as additive, designing of a superhydrophilic and organic solvent resistant membrane, formulation of a composite material that can be converted to porous membrane by phase inversion process using water as non solvent, designing a nanocomposite membrane from water materials for the treatment of petroleum industry wastewater, a novel process of carbon dots mediated coating of graphene oxide on the surface of the membrane to achieve the antimicrobial property to mitigate the biofouling issue.

Poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) used in this work was obtained from the same type of waste cold drink bottles marked with the recyclability code −1 for PET. It is noted that plastic materials for their identification for recycling are coded with different code numbers. Hence, the PET bottles marked with code-1 indicates that the material used to make the bottle is only PET without any blending with other polymers. The bottles collected from different sources were cut into small pieces and washed properly with detergent, followed by normal water, and finally with distilled water. The PET raw materials were then dried at 50 °C in a hot air oven and finally stored for further use. The cellulose was obtained from waste printing papers available in the office. The papers were first cut into small pieces and then washed with hot water, followed by alkali and acid treatment. Finally, the cellulosic raw materials were washed with distilled water, dried properly, and stored for further use. The Polyethylene glycol (PEG)−400 was purchased from TCI (India) Pvt. The acetic acid used was obtained from Merck India. HCl, H2SO4, HNO3, trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), and NaOH were purchased from Avantar Chemicals, India and used as obtained. The graphite flakes used were obtained from SRL chemicals. 25 % glutaraldehyde aqueous solution and chitosan powder were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industries Limited. The molecular weight of the chitosan used is 1526.464 g/mol with a deacetylation degree of at least 80 % after drying.

Waste papers were first soaked in distilled water at 80 °C for an hour, followed by washing. Then the cellulosic materials were treated with 1 M NaOH for 2 h at room temperature and with 1 M HCl for 2 h at 80 °C, followed by washing with distilled water. The required amounts of pretreated cellulosic materials were added to a specific volume of TFA and stirred for 5 h at room temperature in a magnetic stirrer. We used 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5 wt % cellulose in TFA for nanofiber synthesis. TFA breaks the chain structure of cellulose and converts it to the nanofiber form. Finally, cellulose nanofibers form a homogeneous dispersion in TFA. In this way, cellulose nanofibers could be formed by applying a top-down approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PET-cellulose nanofiber composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: After the formation of the cellulose nanofiber in the TFA, a specific amount of PEG-400 and dry PET raw materials were added to the cellulose nanofiber-TFA dispersion and stirred at room temperature for another 2–3 h. The concentration of PET and PEG in the mixture was kept constant and maintained at 10 % and 12 % respectively. The concentration of cellulosic materials was varied to prepare different membrane systems.

The membranes were fabricated from the polymeric mixture by the non-solvent induced phase separation technique (NIPS). In a typical process, the polymeric dispersion mixture was cast on a properly cleaned and dried glass plate of dimension 25 cm × 15 cm using a specially arranged glass rod to maintain a constant thickness of the polymeric layer (⁓270 µm). After casting, the glass plate was immediately immersed in a non-solvent bath containing distilled water. Phase inversion happens immediately and a thin solid polymeric membrane was obtained in the water bath within a few seconds. After around 10 min, the membrane was transferred to a pure distilled water bath. The obtained membrane piece was washed with pure distilled water and dried at room temperature. (
Tables 1 and 2).

Carbon dot was synthesized from a biopolymer called chitosan following an already reported method (Chowdhury et al., 2012). To prepare carbon dots, 0.1 g of chitosan was dissolved in 10 mL 0.1 M acetic acid along with 4 mL glycerol. Then 4.5 mL of 5 M NaOH was added drop wise in the chitosan solution. The chitosan and glycerol present in the solution immediately converted to hydrogel which was collected by filtration using a macroporous substrate. The hydrogel collected was then washed properly and then again dissolved in 100 mL 0.1 M acetic acid. Thus, prepared hydrogel solution was then subjected to microwave radiation using a household microwave oven at power 720 Watt for 5 min. Microwave treatment led to the formation of carbon dots in the solution. Glycerol is an important component for the synthesis of hydrogel from chitosan. Actually, glycerol also becomes a constituent of the carbon dots system. Without addition of glycerol, gelation does not occur properly and carbon dots do not form.

Graphene oxide was synthesized from graphite using a modified Hammer’s method (Gogoi et al., 2017). In this method, 1 g of graphite powder was added to 23 mL of concentrated sulphuric acid at a temperature between 0 and 5 °C. 3 g of KMnO4 was added slowly into the above mixture under constant stirring. The mixture was then stirred at 35 °C for 2 h, followed by the addition of 50 mL of deionized water slowly under vigorous stirring. Then 5 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide was added to this diluted solution that led to the formation of a yellow-colored slurry, which was collected by centrifugation and washed with 1 M HCl followed by acetone until the pH increased above 5. Finally, the as-obtained GO powder was dispersed in deionized water. We diluted the graphene oxide after washing and measured the concentration of graphene oxide in the diluted solution by gravimetric analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon dots-graphene oxide coated membrane
ce:section.ce:para: For carbon dot-graphene oxide coating, we prepared a dispersion of carbon dots and graphene oxide by mixing 50 mL of carbon dots with 50 mL of GO (0.1 mg in 1 mL). The pH of the dispersion was maintained at 3. For coating, we have chosen the PCM2 membrane. The active side of the membrane was allowed to stay in contact with 25 % glutaraldehyde aqueous solution using a sintered glass filtration setup. For this, the membrane was first fixed in the setup and then glutaraldehyde was poured on top of the membrane and allowed to stand for 5 min. After that, the membrane was air-dried to remove the excess water and again fixed in the filtration set up. Then carbon dot graphene oxide dispersion was poured on top of the membrane as described earlier. The system was allowed to stand like this for 30 min. The procedure was repeated two times, and the final membrane was air-dried and preserved for characterization and performance testing.

Oil-water permeation experiment was carried out using a synthetic oil-water emulsion. Synthetic oily water was prepared by mixing 50 mL of diesel with 450 mL of millipore water. To prepare a homogeneous emulsion of oil and water, 0.1 g of sodium lauryl sulphate (SLS) was added. The diesel, deionized water and SLS mixture were stirred for 48 h and allowed to stand for 24 h. The homogeneously mixed oil-water emulsion (OW1) was collected by a separating funnel and preserved for the oil-water permeation experiment. Another emulsion type (OW2) was prepared by the same procedure where crude oil obtained from Numaligarh Refinery Ltd., Golaghat, Assam, India was used as the oil phase to prepare the emulsion. For this, 10 mL of crude oil was mixed with 990 mL of millipore water and 0.2 g of sodium lauryl sulphate as a surfactant. The emulsion size distribution of the crude oil-water emulsion was analyzed and maximum oil droplet was found to be in the range 2–5 µm. The size distribution graph of the oil droplets in the crude oil-water emulsion system is provided in the supplementary information (Fig. S14). No precipitation or coagulation was observed in the prepared oil-water emulsion up to 1 month indicating stability of the oil water emulsion system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oil water permeation experiment through a dead-end permeation setup
ce:section.ce:para: A dead-end filtration setup was used for the separation of oil from synthetic oily water. The membrane-fitted cell consists of two different compartments, each compartment containing approximately 160 mL in volume. In between the two compartments, the flat circular membrane with an effective membrane area of 1.26 × 10−3 m2 was placed over a macroporous nylon support. The feed oil-water emulsion was allowed to enter into the upper compartment of the membrane cell by using a peristaltic pump, and the permeate was collected in the lower compartment and finally in the collector as shown in Fig. 1. The oil-water permeation experiments were carried out at room temperature with different membrane types.

The permeability in L.m−2.h−1 is defined as the volume of permeate V, passing through the membrane, normalized by area, A, at a specific time, t, at a given transmembrane pressure, ΔP, (depicted in Eq. (1)).
(1)
Permeability =
V
A . t . Δ P
For each type of membrane, the permeation study was continued for six hours to get the point of saturation.

Percent oil rejection by the membranes was calculated by the Eq. (2),
(2) Oil rejection (%) = ((Cp-Cf)/Cp) ×100
Where Cp is the concentration of oil in the permeate (mg/L) and Cf is the concentration of oil in the feed (mg/L). In this study, the oil-water separation was monitored using optical microscopy. Both the synthetic oil-water emulsion and the permeate water was observed under the optical microscope to see the presence of oil droplets (Chakrabarty et al., 2010).

There is another method mainly used by industries (ASTM method no. 5520 A) to detect the presence of oil and grease in water (American Society for Testing and Materials, 1991). We applied this technique in the case of the synthetic crude oil emulsion to determine the actual amount of oil and grease going into the emulsion. This technique uses HCl as a demulsifying agent and petroleum ether for the phase separation of oil from water. More simply, in a separating funnel, 1000 mL of the sample was taken, to it, 5 mL 1:1 mixture of HCl in water and 30–35 mL of petroleum ether were added. The mixture was shaken for some time, and after that it was allowed to stand for a few hours for the complete separation of oil and grease. After the oil portion was drained, 10–15 mL of petroleum ether were added to the remaining mixture, shaken and allowed to stand. After the separation, the lower layer was drained and the petroleum ether layer (organic layer) was transferred to an initially weighted dry bowl by filtering it through a filter paper containing anhydrous sodium sulfite. The content was evaporated and the bowl was weighed. The amount of oil and grease in g/mL was measured using Eq. (3).
(3)
Amount
of
oil
and
grease =
final
weight − initial
weight
volume
of
sample
taken

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oil-water permeation experiment: Cross flow filtration
ce:section.ce:para: The oil-water permeation performance of the membranes PCM0 and PCM2 was also studied by using cross-flow permeation equipment as shown in Fig. 2. During the permeation, a membrane with an effective cross-sectional area of 41.4 cm2 was fitted in the membrane cell. The synthesized crude oil-water emulsion mixture was pumped through the membrane cell at a continuous flow rate of 857 mL/minute. The crude oil-water mixture permeated through the membranes PCM0 and PCM2 and was collected in a measuring cylinder at a definite time interval of 1 h to calculate the flux of the membrane. We performed a continuous permeation experiment for seven days through the membrane PCM2, and permeate was collected time to time for oil rejection analysis. We performed UV-Vis spectroscopic analysis of the permeates collected in a time interval of 1 h. For this analysis, we first prepared a calibration curve using crude oil-water mixtures of different concentrations. Using the calibration curve, we could determine the concentration of oily organic materials present in the permeate. Moreover, ASTM method no. 5520 A was also employed to measure the oil and grease content in the permeates (American Society for Testing and Materials, 1991). Moreover, salt concentration effect on the permeability and selectivity of the best membrane PCM2 was also studied in the cross-flow permeation experiment. For this we mixed various weight percentage of NaCl with the crude oil-water emulsion and carried out the permeation through the membrane PCM2 with the same flow rate of 857 mL/min. Variation of flux and selectivity with varying concentration of the salt (NaCl) was analyzed.

Confirmation of nanofiber formation and morphological as well as microstructural characteristics of the membranes were observed by the TEM and SEM. TEM images of the nanofibers were taken under a JEOL TEM-2100 plus model. TEM images were obtained by using a sample prepared by drop casting a diluted cellulosic nanofiber solution on a copper grid and silicon wafer, respectively, followed by drying. For SEM analysis the membranes were cut into a dimension of 2 mm × 2 mm. Prior to the analysis the membrane samples were dried overnight in a vacuum drier at room temperature. After that the membranes were dipped in a liquid nitrogen chamber for at least 1 h and then sputtered with gold for making the membranes conducting for capturing the images. The surface and cross-sectional images were observed under a field emission scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss Sigma VP microscope). The electrons were emitted from a filament and collimated into a beam in the electron source. The beam was then focused on the sample surface by a set of lenses in the electron column. The samples were observed in different resolutions. To investigate the elemental composition of the membranes energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was employed that was coupled with the scanning electron microscope. AFM analysis of the membranes was performed to study the surface topography using AFM Nanomagnetics' hpAFM analyzer. All the measurements were carried out in tapping mode for sample size of 1 cm × 1 cm for an effective area of 2 × 2 µm2 at 5 different places and the average was reported.

The presence of functional groups and their interaction were studied using FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of the membranes were collected by a Perkin Elmer FTIR instrument in ATR mode. The spectral range was set between 4000 cm−1 and 400 cm−1 and 16 scans were done. The FTIR spectrometer has a resolution of 3.1 µm. ssNMR analysis of the cellulose and cellulose nanofiber were done in a Bruker 400 MHz NMR spectrometer. Prior to the analysis the samples were grounded to fine powders and kept in desiccators overnight. Thermostability of the membranes was studied using a Perkin Elmer 4000 instrument, and the analysis was performed in the range of 35 − 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and with a nitrogen flow rate of 20 mL/min. To study the mechanical properties of the membranes, we used a universal testing machine ("Tinius Olsen 5ST" model) with a load cell of 2.5 kN capacity. The speed of the testing was maintained at 5 mm/min. For tensile strength measurement, the membranes were cut into rectangular pieces of dimensions of 10 cm × 2.5 cm and placed in desiccators before the mechanical strength was analyzed. The test was conducted at a relative humidity of 75 % and at room temperature. The samples were kept in this condition for at least 1 h. Contact angle of the membranes were measured by using a contact angle analyzer (Model: DM-501, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd.). The average pore size and surface area of the membranes was determined using an BET analyzer (Quantachrome Instruments version 5.21). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms for all the membranes were done on the BET analyzer (Quantachrome Instruments version 5.21). Prior to this analysis, the specific membranes were cut into small pieces and degassing of the same was done in vacuum at 110 0C for 6 h by using a XeriPrep degasser.

The elemental analysis as well as the XPS spectra were obtained from the Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ instrument. The type of source in the XPS analysis is Aluminum K-α, the power of the XPS instrument is 1486.6 eV, the calibration standard was set at C-C bond binding energy of 284.8 eV, the number of scans performed was 10 with a pass energy of 50 eV during elemental analysis and 200 eV during the survey; the fitting constraint was set as + 0.6 eV. The fluorescence study of carbon dots was done using the instrument ‘Fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS) HORIBA Instruments: FLUOROLOG-3 and the UV-Vis analysis of the permeates was done using Analytic Jena SPECORD® 210 Plus. Water absorption analysis was performed for all the membranes. For this small rectangular piece (2 cm × 1 cm) of the membranes PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 were cut and kept in a desiccator overnight. All the samples were weighed using an analytical balance and the constant weight was recorded. The membranes were then soaked in distilled water taken in small sample bottles and kept for 24 h. Weight of each wet membrane piece was recorded after removing the water droplets attached to the surface. Water absorption percentage was calculated by using the following formula.
(4)
% Water
a bsorption =
W ( w e t ) − W ( d r y )
W ( d r y )
× 100
where, W(wet) and W(dry) are the weight of the membranes in the wet and dry state respectively.

The porosity measurement of all the membrane samples was determined using the gravimetric method (An et al., 2019). For this, the membranes PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 were cut into square pieces of dimension 4 cm × 4 cm and kept in desiccators overnight. All the samples were weighed using an analytical balance, and the constant weight was recorded. Then, the membrane samples were dipped in isopropanol for 30 min, followed by another dipping in a mixture of 50 % isopropanol in deionized water for 30 min, and finally in deionized water for 8 h in order to ensure complete wetting of the membrane sample. The fully wetted membrane pieces were weighed after removing the superficial water from the surface of the membrane pieces. The percent porosity was calculated using Eq. (5).
(5)
ɛ =
W ( s ) − W ( d )
V ( m )
× 100
where, W(s) is the weight of the membrane after 8 hours of soaking in water and W(d) is the weight of the dry membrane and V(m), volume of the membrane sample = (area × thickness) of the membrane sample.

Capillary condensation flow porometer (PMI, Model CCFP-5A) was used to determine the membrane porosity, which is highly effective for the transport properties of membranes (Baruah et al., 2012). The principle of analysis is mentioned in the supporting materials.

The antibacterial activity of the membranes PCM2 and PCMC was studied with one Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial test pathogen. The bacterial pathogens that were used in the experiment were obtained from the Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC) and the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The bacterial cultures, Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 11632, which is a gram-positive bacteria, and Escherichia coli ATCC 11229, which is a gram-negative bacteria, were grown overnight at 37 °C at 150 rpm in a shaking condition for 18 h in Mueller Hinton broth. 80 μL of overnight grown bacterial suspension was poured and spread with the help of a sterile spreader over aseptically prepared solid Mueller Hinton agar plates. The membrane samples PCM2 and PCMC were cut into small square pieces and kept on top of the solid Mueller-Hinton agar plates. The plates were incubated overnight at 37 °C for optimal growth.

A robust, organic solvent-resistant membrane could be designed using waste PET materials by the recycling of used plastic bottles, whereas the hydrophilicity of the membranes could be achieved by using cellulose nanofibers obtained from waste paper. The scheme of fabrication of the membranes is shown in Scheme 1.

The waste paper was first washed and treated properly as described in the experimental section. The pre-treated cellulosic material was then dispersed in trifluoroacetic acid (TFA). After stirring mechanically for 5 h at room temperature, the cellulose materials were converted to the nanofiber form. Formation of the nanofibers is vividly perceptible from the TEM images ( Fig. 3A). Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) is one of the nonaqueous solvents for cellulose, and can dissolve both native and regenerated cellulose at room temperature (Hasegawa et al., 1992). During solubilization, cellulose is trifluoroacetylated selectively at the C6-hydroxyl groups in the TFA solution. However; in nature, cellulose does not occur as individual molecules. It occurs as an assembly of individual molecules called fiber (Habibi et al., 2010). Hence, in the dissolution process at a particular point, the cellulosic materials produce nanofibers. In this case, we optimized the time required for the formation of cellulose nanofibers (CNF) and maintained it very carefully. FTIR analysis of the cellulose nanofiber confirms that cellulose retains its structure after its conversion to the nanofiber form (Fig. S2(C)).

Following the formation of the nanofiber, a specific amount of PEG-400 and PET were added to the nanofiber dispersion one by one. PET gets dissolved in the TFA solution, forming a homogeneous dispersion of CNF-PET in TFA. The solution was then cast on a glass plate, and the membranes were fabricated by the phase inversion technique using distilled water as the non-solvent. Finally, we obtained porous membranes as seen from the SEM images [Fig. 3(B, C)]. The membranes thus prepared were coded as PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 for varying cellulose content in the membranes, viz. 0 %, 1 %, 1.5 %, 2 % and 2.5 % respectively. The thickness of the membranes varied from 100 to 150 µm. Initially, we tried to fabricate a PET membrane without PEG by using water as non-solvent. However, membranes from only PET without PEG couldn’t be formed. On the other hand, as the cellulosic materials were in the nanofiber form, they also failed to form homogenous solid membranes. A smooth and stable membrane could be obtained only after the use of polyethylene glycol. PEG-400 has a crucial role in the formation of PET-CNF composite membranes. It acts as a plasticizer as well as a compatibilizer inside the polymeric matrix (Ferrarezi et al., 2013; Ahmed et al., 2019; Domjan et al., 2009; Tajeddin, 2015; Konwar et al., 2015, 2018; Chevalier et al., 2008; Orsolini et al., 2015). Absence of PEG caused the coagulation of CNFs during the phase inversion process and thus inhibited the formation of smooth membranes. We also observed the effect of the molecular weight of PEG on the membrane formation process. Finally, we optimized 12 % of PEG-400 as the optimum percentage and molecular weight for the fabrication of smooth membranes. Fig. S1 shows photographic images showing the differences in surface morphology of the membrane without PEG and after the addition of PEG-400. The presence of CNF has a significant role in the pore formation process. As seen from the SEM images [Fig. 3(B, C)], the membrane without CNF has very few pores on its surface. The number of pores on the surface increased significantly after the addition of 1 % nanofibers. Cellulose, being hydrophilic in nature acts as a pore forming agent for water, acting as non-solvent against the polymeric matrix system (Bagheripour et al., 2016). The fibrous nature of cellulosic nanofibers may also have some effect on the porous structure of the composite membranes (Zhou et al., 2012). We could see an excessive number of pores on the surface of the membrane with 2 % CNF (PCM2). However, with the increase in CNF concentration (for 2.5 % CNF), the pore size on the surface of the membranes becomes smaller, as depicted by the SEM images. This decrease in pore size may be due to of the compactness as well as an increase in viscosity of the CNF-PET solution with the increase in nanofiber concentration (Kim et al., 2018).

The FTIR analysis was used to determine the different types of functional groups present in the membrane matrix part [ Fig. 4(a, b)]. The bare PET membrane (PCM0) shows sharp peaks at 1102 and 1261 cm−1 that are attributed to the –C-O stretching. The peak at 1719 cm−1 arises from the –CO stretching, while the 2966 cm−1 peak is because of the –C-H stretching. In the case of PET-CNF composite (PCM2), the peaks for the same functional groups are at 1110 cm−1, 1254 cm−1, 1722 cm−1 and 2962 cm−1 respectively. The major difference between the PCM0 and PCM2 membranes lies in the peak position and peak intensity for –O–H stretching. The –O–H stretching for the PCM0 membrane occurs at a very low intensity at 3635 cm−1. This is because of the terminal –O–H groups present in PET and PEG. On the other hand, the peak intensity of the –O–H group increases significantly in the case of the PCM2 membrane, which is attributed to the presence of an excessive amount of –O–H group in the cellulosic. Moreover, from EDS analysis of membrane PCM0 and PCM2 (Fig. 3D) it was observed that the weight percent of oxygen in the case of PCM0 is less than that in the case of PCM2 which was also attributed to the presence of more hydrophilic OH groups in the case of PCM2. With the increase in the number of –O–H groups, the intra-molecular as well as intermolecular hydrogen bonding also increases, leading to the shifting of FTIR absorption for –O–H stretching to 3423 cm−1 (Kim et al., 2018; Johra et al., 2014). The -OH functional group of cellulose nanofibers can interact with the oxygen atoms present in the ester and –O–H groups of PET through polar-polar or hydrogen bonding interactions. Thus, we can expect very good compatibility between these two components of the membrane. The compatibility was experienced through the homogeneous mixing of these two materials during the fabrication process and is also evident from the cross-sectional SEM images after fabrication. ssNMR analysis of the cellulose and cellulose nanofiber gives interesting results. We could observe a strong similarity in the 13C NMR spectrum for cellulose and cellulose nanofiber. 13C ssNMR spectra obtained for the extracted cellulose and cellulose nanofiber have been shown in the supplementary materials (Fig. S3) of the revised manuscript.

The presence of various polar functional groups in the PCM2 membrane was confirmed by XPS analysis [Fig. 4(c, d)]. In the deconvoluted high resolution XPS spectra of C1s for the membrane sample PCM2 (Fig. 4c), the peaks at 284 and 284.7 eV are attributed to the –C–C and –CC carbons, respectively. Peaks at 286 and 288.6 eV are responsible for the –C–O and –O–CO groups. On the other hand, in the O1s spectra (Fig. 4d), peaks at 531, 531.8 and 532.9 eV are attributed to the –O–H, –O–C and –OC groups (Fujimoto et al., 2016; Gonzalez et al., 2008; Drewniak et al., 2016; Jung and Bhushan, 2006).

The presence of cellulose nanofiber inside the PET matrix greatly influenced the surface properties of the membrane. The surface property of the membrane can be represented by the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of the membrane surface, which is in turn represented by the contact angle value. Contact angle (CA) is the angle made by a liquid drop with its substrate plane. When this contact angle is measured with respect to water, then the surface is said to be hydrophobic for CA value ˃ 90°, and it is called hydrophilic when the CA value is < 90°. The hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of a surface depends mainly on two factors, viz. (i) surface polarity and (ii) surface roughness. The surface polarity of a material is controlled by the ratio of polar groups/elements to non-polar groups/elements. Surface roughness is another factor that can improve both properties depending on the original material property. If θ is the CA for a smooth surface, then for θ ˃ 90°, with increasing surface roughness, the CA value increases, and on the other hand, for θ < 90°, with increasing surface roughness, the CA value decreases (Gonzalez et al., 2008).

The contact angle study of the membranes showed very interesting results ( Fig. 5a). The PET membrane (PCM-0) without any cellulose nanofiber showed a CA value of 58°. After the addition of 1 % CNF, the contact angle value drops drastically to 37° and up to 31° after the addition of 2.5 % CNF. This decrease in CA value is mainly due to the increase in polar groups in the membrane matrix. Surface roughness might further enhance this hydrophilicity. This surface roughness factor was evaluated from the AFM images ( Fig. 6). The roughness parameter (Ra) was found to be 4.6 for the membrane PCM0, while an increased value of 5.7 was obtained for the membrane PCM2. Moreover, it was further observed that while in the case of only PET membrane, the water drop remains as it is with time, for the CNF containing membranes, the water drop gradually gets absorbed with time. The time required for total absorption of the water drop by the membrane decreases with the increase in CNF concentration. Both the hydrophilicity as well as the pore sizes of the membrane controls this absorption behavior (Liu et al., 2021). At up to 2 % cellulose nanofiber concentration, the hydrophilicity of the membrane is predominantly responsible for this absorption behavior, above 2 % cellulose nanofiber; pore size predominates in the absorption time of the water droplet by the membrane. The porosity of the membrane samples was found to be approximately 64.95 %, 73.73 %, 77.5 %, 89.09 % and 85.63 % for PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 respectively. The porosity of the membranes gradually increased from PCM0 to PCM2 and after that a significant decrease in porosity was observed in the case of PCM2.5, which may be due to the agglomeration of CNFs over the pores after the optimum concentration. We have also determined the percent absorption of water by the membranes. Because of their porous structure, all the membranes show high water absorption behavior. The water absorption of the membranes was also found in accordance with the data obtained in the contact angle analysis and porosity. Water absorption values were found to be around 200 %, 236 %, 350 %, 361 % and 333 % for the membranes PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 respectively. The lower water absorption for PCM2.5 compared to PCM2 is due to its lower porosity as described above. However, the SEM images clearly show that pore density on the surface of the membrane PCM0 is very low, whereas it is very significant on the PCM2 membrane surface.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm for the membranes PCM0, PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 gives the hysteresis loop, which matches with the type IV isotherm that stands for the mesoporosity present in the membrane structure (Borah et al., 2022). The nitrogen adsorption isotherm of the membranes has been shown in Fig. S6 of the supplementary material. The surface pore size distribution of the membrane was analysed by using the imageJ software from the SEM images of the membranes (Fig. S9). The membrane PCM0 has maximum number of pores in the range 20–25 nm, PCM1 has maximum number of pores in the range 30–40 nm, Likewise PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 has maximum number of pores in the range 25–30 nm, 70–80 nm and 30–35 nm respectively. The bulk pore size distribution (including the surface and inner pores) of the membranes was determined by the DFT method using BET analysis for pore distribution, which has been presented in Fig. S7 of the supplementary material. BET surface area and pore surface area of the designed membranes were also analyzed and listed in Table S1. We could observe highest pore surface area in case of membrane PCM2 (Table S1) which is also in accordance to the results obtained from the porosity measurement and water absorption analysis of the membranes.

In addition to the BET analysis, capillary condensation flow porometer (CCFP) was also employed to measure the overall pore size of the membranes as it is the advanced method to determine the through holes, blind holes as well as the pore-throats (the narrowest point in a through hole) present in the membranes. The results have been depicted in Fig. S8 of the supplementary materials. The results are nearly similar to the results obtained from the BET analysis and SEM analysis of the membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent resistant property of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Another very significant property of our designed membranes is their solvent resistance ability. Although the solvent resistance ability of PET and cellulose is well established, we have tested the behavior of our membranes after regeneration in the non-solvent bath. The solvent resistance property of the membranes was compared with the polysulfone membranes synthesized in our laboratory. Polysulfone-based membranes are one of the most commonly used membranes nowadays (Gogoi et al., 2021a, 2021b). Fig. 6(d) shows the photographic images of the membranes soaked in different types of commonly available organic solvents for 24 h. The PET-CNF (PCM2) membranes seemed to retain their structure in all the solvents, while the polysulfone membranes got dissolved in almost all the solvents just after a few minutes of dipping. Photographic images of the membranes soaked for 24 h and after taking them out of the solvents are shown in Fig. S10 in the supplementary material.

The mechanical strength and stability of the membranes were studied using a universal testing machine and evaluated in terms of tensile strength value. The tensile strength of a substance can be defined as the maximum force per unit area required to break that sample before rupture. Because of the high strength of PET material, the membranes fabricated by using only PET (PCM0) possess good mechanical strength, with a tensile strength value of 2.33 MPa. Although a decrease in tensile strength value was observed after the incorporation of cellulose nanofiber into the PET matrix, all the membranes fabricated possessed sufficient mechanical strength to serve the purpose. The decrease in tensile strength is due to the highly porous structure formed after incorporation of CNF as can be observed from the SEM images of the cross section of the membranes (Fig. 3C). The tensile strength values of PCM1, PCM1.5, PCM2 and PCM2.5 were found to be 1.19, 0.95, 0.86 and 0.76 MPa respectively.

The thermal stability of the membranes was studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The membranes showed very good thermal stability. PCM0 membrane shows its initial degradation temperature at around 385 °C while the PCM2 membrane sample shows its degradation starting at around 350 °C. The early elimination of large amounts of hydroxyl groups present in the cellulosic material may be responsible for the lower degradation temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of the membranes for oil water separation
Performance evaluation using dead end permeation experiment
ce:section.ce:para: The fabricated membranes were successfully used for the separation of oil droplets from an oil water mixture. For this test, we used both refined and crude oil. The oil-water emulsion was prepared as described in the experimental section. Typically, 10 % of diesel and 1 % of crude oil with respect to distilled water were used to prepare the oil-water emulsion using sodium lauryl sulfate as a surfactant. However, the whole amount of oil does not take part in the emulsification. Therefore, we separated the extra oil by using a separating funnel. The actual amount of oil that took part in the emulsification process was ∼ 600 mg/L. The crude oil-water emulsion was also found to contain around 143 mg/L of other suspended solid matter in it. The amount of crude oil present in the emulsion was determined using a gravimetric method described in the experimental section. This method is used by the oil industries for determination of the presence of oil and grease in water. However, due to the low amount of oil entering the emulsified state, which is also visible in the microscopic images [ Fig. 7(C, D)], this method could not be used to determine the diesel oil content in diesel oil-water emulsion.

The fabricated membranes were tested for oil water separation using a dead-end filtration setup, as shown in Fig. 7B. The membranes showed very good water permeability during the separation of oil-water emulsion, although the permeability was different for different types of membranes with varying percentages of CNF.

The membrane without CNF (PCM0) showed very low water permeability, while the maximum permeability was obtained for the PCM2 membrane. Both surface properties and pore size could be responsible for this difference in permeability. The membrane PCM0 possesses a very small number of pores on its surface with comparatively higher hydrophobicity, hence it shows minimum permeability. On the other hand, the membrane PCM2, with higher porous structure and having very high hydrophilic property, shows maximum permeability. The permeability of the membranes also varied with respect to the emulsion system. Since the crude oil emulsion system has a higher amount of oil and grease content along with some other solid substances, the permeability for this system was found to be lower compared to that of the diesel oil emulsion system, which is a pure one. The maximum permeability for the PCM2 membranes goes up to around 98 Lm−2h−1 in the case of diesel oil emulsion while it reaches up to around 84 Lm−2h−1 in the case of crude oil emulsion at 1.5 bar pressure. The maximum pressure we could apply in our system was 2 bar. We observed variation of permeability through the membrane with change in pressure i.e., 1, 1.5 and 2 bar ( Fig. 8(c)). However, we did not observe any change in selectivity of the membrane within this pressure limit.

The oil removal efficiency of the membranes was confirmed with the help of optical microscopy. The microscopic images of the emulsion system and the permeate were observed under a microscope at 100X magnification. The emulsion systems clearly show the presence of emulsified oil droplets of varying sizes, while, on the other hand, the permeate was totally free of any oil droplets. In the case of the crude oil emulsion system, we also carried out the gravimetric method as mentioned earlier (ASTM method no. 5520 A) to check for the presence of any oil or grease in the permeate (American Society for Testing and Materials, 1991). This test also confirmed that the permeate is devoid of any oil, grease or solid content. As observed in the optical microscopic images, the oil or grease contents are present in the emulsified droplet form, so the separation is mainly based on the size exclusion principle (Duman et al., 2022a). It was further observed that while water drops are easily absorbed by the membrane PCM2, drops of an oily substance are not at all absorbed by the membrane (Fig. 7A). Thus, this selective absorption of water can further enhance the separation process.

Further confirmation regarding the retention ability of the membranes was obtained by the UV-Vis spectroscopic analysis. For this, we collected UV-Vis spectrum of the different concentrations of oil-water emulsion and prepared a calibration curve (Fig. S11 (b)). Using this calibration curve, the concentration of the organic contaminants in the permeate was determined. This UV-Vis absorption study revealed around 98 % removal of oil based organic contaminants from the feed solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation using cross flow permeation experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Cross flow permeation study was also carried out for the membranes PCM0 and PCM2. Permeability or flux of PCM0 membrane (without cellulose) was found to be very minimum (⁓ 6.5 L.m−2.h−1). PCM2 membrane with 2 % cellulose content showed maximum permeability of 20.3 L.m−2.h−1. To check the durability of the membrane, we performed the crossflow permeation experiment continuously upto 7 days. The membrane could perform smoothly with constant rejection of ⁓ 97 % till the 7th day of observation with a slight decline in flux (11.4 L.m−2.h−1). The membrane was observed under SEM after continuously using it for 7 days which is shown in supplementary materials (Fig. S13). The SEM images showed some deposition of solid maters on its surface although the surface pores were not seemed to be blocked completely. Deposition of such solid matters are responsible for the decline of flux in long term operation.

When analyzed for oil and grease content in the permeate using the oil and grease content determination method (ASTM method no. 5520 A) we found almost zero oil and grease content present in the permeate (American Society for Testing and Materials, 1991). Moreover, the retention ability of the membranes was also analyzed by the UV-Vis spectroscopic analysis of the permeates as described above. UV-Vis spectra obtained for the permeates collected in a definite time interval has been depicted in Fig. S9 (c). A constant rejection of 97–98 % was observed throughout the study.

Effect of salt (NaCl) concentration on the permeability and selectivity of the best membrane PCM2 was also carried out as petroleum wastewater may also contain various type of salts. No significant change in permeability and selectivity for oil water separation was observed. This may be due to the high stability of the PET and cellulose nanofiber as well as very good compatibility between these two materials in the membrane. The results have been presented in Fig. S12 of the supplementary materials.

A good compatibility between two polymers can only be observed when the difference in the solubility parameters of the individual polymers is in between the range 0.42–1.47 (Joule-cm3)1/2 (Golicha and Katia, 2020). Using Eq. (3) given in the supplementary materials, the solubility parameter for PET and cellulose were calculated. Solubility parameter (δ) for PET was found to be 32.52 (Joule-cm3)1/2 while it was 33.53 (Joule-cm3)1/2 for cellulose. That means a difference in solubility parameter of the two polymers PET and cellulose is 1.01 (Joule-cm3)1/2. Therefore, as observed from experimental analysis a very good compatibility between the two polymers can also be verified with theoretical calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Addition of antimicrobial activity to the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial property of the membrane was achieved by coating the membrane with carbon dots rooted in graphene oxide sheets. We used the PCM2 membrane for coating because of its high water absorption capability. For this, the active side of the membrane was first grafted with glutaraldehyde as described in the experimental section. With high water absorption property as well as the presence of polar groups on its surface, the membrane could easily get attached to glutaraldehyde. It is noteworthy to mention here that for the PCM0 membrane, absorption of glutaraldehyde was not possible, and hence we could not apply this technique to achieve the antimicrobial property. Glutaraldehyde can easily form covalent bonds with the amine groups present in the chitosan molecule as shown in scheme S1. Therefore, it has become a commonly used crosslinking agent for chitosan for the preparation of chitosan hydrogel (Konwar et al., 2019). ( Fig. 9) Glutaraldehyde grafted membrane was then allowed to come into contact with a carbon dots (CDs) rooted graphene oxide solution prepared in 0.1 M acetic acid. Thus, we obtained a uniform coating of CDs rooted graphene oxide on the surface of the membrane, which was observed under SEM. The synthesis of carbon dots and graphene oxides is described in the experimental section. The synthesized carbon dots showed their characteristic fluorescent behavior under UV light. We have also collected the fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots solution. Here we observed its characteristic spectral behavior, i.e., first increasing and then diminishing intensity pattern on increasing the excitation wavelength between 320 nm and 380 nm (Tajeddin, 2015). DLS spectrum ( Fig. 10B) for size measurement confirms the formation of carbon dots with size < 10 nm. Measurement of zeta potential by the DLS technique indicates a positive surface charge of around + 38 mV on carbon dots and a negative surface charge of around ̶ 21 mV on graphene oxide (Scheme S1). Because of this large difference in surface charge value, a stable electrostatic interaction can be expected between the carbon dots and the graphene oxide surface. This interaction was proven when we obtained a very uniform coating of graphene oxide on the surface of the membrane. The carbon dots attached to the graphene oxide surface could form bonds with the glutaraldehyde present on the membrane surface and hence bring the graphene oxide onto the membrane surface. After this coating, the tensile strength of the membrane (PCMC) increased to 1.6 MPa up from 0.76 MPa for the bare one (PCM2).

Antimicrobial activity of the membrane (PCMC) was studied against two bacterial species, viz., Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, with gram negative and gram-positive strains, respectively. The antimicrobial activity was also compared with the membrane without coating (PCM2). The coated membranes seemed to show resistance against both the bacterial strains, while the bare membrane did not show any activity. A thin clear zone surrounding the PCMC membrane samples was observed for both the bacterial colonies on agar medium. Then antimicrobial activity of carbon dots is because of its source material, chitosan. In mild acidic conditions, chitosan acquires a positive charge because of the protonation of its –NH2 group to –NH3 +. This positive charge of chitosan can interact with the negatively charged materials present on the bacterial surface. For gram negative bacterial strain, the cell wall consists of a thick peptidoglycan layer that contains negatively charged teichoic acids. Thus, by electrostatic interaction, chitosan can hydrolyze the peptidoglycans, leading to the leakage of intracellular components. On the other hand, in the case of the gram-negative bacterial species, chitosan can bring about changes in the permeability of the outer envelope by forming an ionic type of bond. These changes lead to the prevention of the transport of nutrients into the cell and build up an internal osmotic pressure. Ultimately, the cell dies due to a lack of nutrients. Moreover, quaternary ammonium groups (R-NH3 +) present on the chitosan backbone can compete with the divalent metal ions, such as Ca2+ and Mg2+, present in the bacterial cell wall for binding with the polyanions (Gogoi et al., 2021a). Graphene oxide, on the other hand, is a well-established antimicrobial agent. The antimicrobial activity of graphene oxide is because of the induced oxidative stress, leading to the oxidation of proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids and ultimately resulting in membrane damage and cell death. Some reports also mention the observation of cell rupture on direct contact with the sharp blade like edges of graphene sheets (Sahariah and Masson, 2017; Konwar et al., 2016; Perreault et al., 2015). Thus, the combined effect of both these nanomaterials inhibits the growth of bacterial colonies close to the membrane surface.

When we used these coated membranes for treating the crude oil-water emulsion, we observed slightly higher retention ability compared to the bare one, as indicated by the UV-spectroscopy study. Coating of graphene oxide on a membrane surface form wrinkle-like structures perceptible from the SEM images. Water or other solvents can pass through the nano-channels present in between two graphene oxide sheets (Seo et al., 2018; Othman et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2021). Coating of carbon dots and graphene oxide improved the tensile strength of the membrane significantly along with the addition of antimicrobial property to the membrane. However, in this strategy, we had to compromise a little with the permeability of the membrane. Petroleum industry wastewater contains numerous microbial pathogens. Therefore, biofouling may occur which will affect in the performance of the membrane. Hence antimicrobial property is very important for membrane-based treatment of wastewater. Our strategy can also be applied for other types of hydrophilic membranes and surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison of the membrane PCM2 with PCM2 (Methanol)
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the properties and performance of the best performing membrane PCM2 prepared using water and methanol (PCM2 (Methanol)) as non solvent, we considered the surface properties, mechanical strength as well as permeability and selectivity of the membranes.

Contact angle analysis of the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) has been shown in Fig. S14(A) of the supplementary materials. Water Contact angle of the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) was found to be 530 which is greater than that observed for the membrane PCM2. Moreover, the time for total absorption of the water droplet in case of PCM2 (Methanol) was longer than PCM2. These two observations clearly confirms that membrane PCM2 (Methanol) is less hydrophilic than membrane PCM2. Similarly, SEM analysis of the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) gives interesting results (Fig. S14(B) in supplementary materials). Though the surface porosity of the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) is comparable to that of the membrane PCM2 but bulk porosity in the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) is much lower which could be clearly observed from the cross-sectional SEM image of the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) (Fig. S14 C). It was already reported that cellulose nanofiber act as a pore forming agent while using water as non solvent. But when methanol is used as non solvent the cellulose nanofiber cannot have any significant effect in the pore formation process because of the lower polarity of methanol (polarity index of water = 10.2, polarity index of methanol = 5.1) (Kleiman et al., 2016). The less water absorption capacity of membrane PCM2 (Methanol) is due to its lesser porosity as compared to PCM2. It is assumed that due to the high polarity of water compared to methanol attracts more CNF to the surface of the membrane while using water as non solvent. Therefore, lower initial contact angle value and hence higher hydrophilicity was observed in case of PCM2 compared to PCM2 (Methanol).

Tensile strength analysis of the membrane also gives interesting results. The membrane PCM2 (Methanol) possess low tensile strength (0.329 MPa) as compared to the PCM2. We have already assumed that due to high polarity index of water compared to methanol the presence of CNFs on the surface is larger than in the bulk of the membrane in case of PCM2. While in case of PCM2 (Methanol), as the CNFs mostly remain in the bulk of the membrane making the bulk of the membrane more fibrous than PCM2 and hence will reduce the tensile strength.

Separation performance of both the membranes i.e. PCM2 (Methanol) and PCM2 for crude oil water emulsion was studied and found that a maximum flux of 65.6 Lm−2h−1 was observed for PCM2 (Methanol) while it was 84 Lm−2h−1 for membrane PCM2. Moreover, the flux decline was found to be similar in case of both the membranes. Similarly, oil rejection of more than 97 % was also observed for the membrane PCM2 (Methanol) and this remains same throughout the performance study which is almost same for PCM2 membrane. The results have been shown in Fig. S15 of the supplementary materials.

From these observations it is clear that the membrane formed using cellulose nanofiber as additive and water as the non-solvent performs superior than that fabricated with methanol as the non-solvent. In case of PCM2 membrane, cellulose nanofiber significantly played an important role in pore formation throughout the membrane while it was not observed in case of PCM2 (Methanol) membrane. Replacement of costly methanol with water as the non-solvent is definitely a key success in the study as lots of non-solvent is required during the membrane fabrication in the NIPS process. In addition to this, use of cellulose nanofiber will decrease the total plastic volume in the membrane.

Finally, we can conclude that we have successfully fabricated a kind of superhydrophillic membrane system using waste PET bottles and cellulosic paper. The cellulosic nanofibers present inside the PET matrix act as pore-forming agents in aqueous media, making it possible to use water as a non-solvent in the membrane fabrication process. Hence, by replacing methanol as the non-solvent for PET-based membranes reported in the available literature with water, a huge reduction in the production cost of such membranes could be achieved. Moreover, improvement in hydrophilicity also leads to an increase in permeability. Hydrophilicity was increased to such an extent that the membranes absorbed the water drops falling on them within a few seconds. Although, the mechanical strength was reduced after the incorporation of cellulose nanofiber, the membranes showed sufficient strength to serve their purpose. Considering the surface porosity, hydrophilicity, and hence the permeability, the membrane PCM2 containing 2 % cellulose nanofibers could be considered as the membrane with the optimum formulation. This membrane also showed very high thermal stability with a first degradation temperature of 350 °C in thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The PCM2 membrane showed a maximum permeability of ∼ 98 Lm−2h−1 against diesel oil-water emulsion system and ∼ 84 Lm−2h−1 against the crude oil-water system. The membrane was also found to be highly durable for oil water separation which has been established by long term cross-flow permeation experiment. The membrane is capable of effective removal of oil, grease as well as solid contents from an oil-water system. It is also possible to provide antimicrobial activity to such composite membranes to address the biofouling issue. Thus, our designed membrane can be a low-cost solution to fabricate highly effective membranes for treatment of petroleum industry wastewater paving a way to convert waste to wealth, which will in fact boost the circular economy.

ce:section.ce:para: Photographic images of PET-CNF membranes with and without PEG; FTIR spectra of Carbon dots and Graphene Oxides; FTIR spectra of cellulose nanofiber; 13C NMR spectra of cellulose and cellulose nanofiber; high resolution XPS spectra of carbon dots; XRD spectra of graphite and graphene oxide; Schematic representation of coating the membrane surface with carbon dots rooted graphene oxide and mechanism of bonding between glutaraldehyde and carbon dots; DLS spectra for Zeta potential of carbon dots and graphene oxide in 0.1 M acetic acid; nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherm; pore distribution of the designed membranes by DFT method; surface pore distribution analysis of the membranes from SEM images by using imageJ software; Solvent resistant behavior of membrane PCM2; UV-Vis spectroscopic analysis of the permeates; effect of salt concentration on performance of the membrane PCM2; oil-droplet size distribution from microscopic image; fabrication of membrane PCM2 (Methanol), SEM analysis, contact angle analysis and tensile strength analysis of membrane PCM2 (Methanol); performance evaluation of membrane PCM2(Methanol).
Designing a superhydrophilic and organic solvent resistant membrane from waste plastic bottles and papers for treatment of petroleum industry waste-water.

Petroleum industry wastewater contains a high level of crude oil along with other organic substances. Being carcinogenic in nature, such organic substances become hazardous for human health. They can also reduce soil fertility and cause immense harm to aquatic life. In this work, we have successfully developed an organic solvent resistant high performance membrane using waste PET materials with the help of cellulose nanofiber for the removal of oily substances from wastewater produced by the petroleum industry. Our work is also an endeavor to develop high quality products from waste plastic materials to minimize the pollution caused by these plastic waste materials.
